theme: The Punctual Receptionist and the International Match
text:
Today, everyone at the international sports club knew that the chess final was imminent. I am the receptionist there, a punctual and qualified worker, and basically my job is to count guests, sign forms, and see that no one feels like an illiterate outsider. There is always a chance that some workaholic designer or a conservative baron will arrive late, but I dare not let the schedule deteriorate.

The champion's opponent this year was a first-rate proletarian player from a distant commonwealth, a man of dogged, magnanimous spirit. He had survived bronchitis as a child, broken his femur in a fall, and once seemed a victim of every misfortune. People used to suspect his nerve would collapse, that his form was rotten and his confidence insoluble. Yet he refused to dodge the contention; with quiet solidarity among his fans, he turned each match into a marvel.

Before the storm of the final, the hall was tense. I deduct nothing from the drama when I say the air felt drowsy yet electric, as if some omen hung above the platform. A series of officials, diplomatic and needful of order, tried to clarify the rules and modulate the rising uproar. One absent-minded count kept toying with a walnut, while an aunt of the champion peeled a butterfly-shaped pastry and offered it around. A premium had been paid for the freight that brought the engraved trophy, and rejection of any late entry was final; the committee would shelve no complaint.

The game itself was an infinite pleasure to watch. Each player would stretch his thinking, tense as a spring, and the stimulus of the clock made every move ragged with risk. Protein bars and luxury chocolates lay untouched. When the challenger forced a merge of his rooks and a sudden sign of weakness appeared in the champion's defence, a ridiculous, joyful uproar broke out. It was pure fun, intelligible even to a kid who had wandered in from the zoo trip next door.

The pertinence of the result was clear to all. The flight home would carry a new hero. As the players signed the scoresheet, I felt the enjoyment of an ordinary day made extraordinary. A dependant of the old champion, drowsy in his seat, woke to applaud. Some said the dynasty had ended; others insisted it had merely undergone a consolidation. There was no conspiracy, no profane trick, no parody of sport, only a clean victory. The motorway outside filled with cars, and as the pulp of the day's newspapers spun off the presses with my photograph in the corner, I let myself, for once, simply marvel and seclude my doubts. Because the existing engine could hardly start, we left it under a blanket in the barn. I would peel an orange and write a letter in a calm voice, careful not to harass anyone. A scientific spirit of stewardship should permeate every page rather than leave it blank, yet a child's toy lay forgotten where the cold would permeate the boards.
translation:
今天，国际体育俱乐部里人人都知道，国际象棋决赛即将到来。我是那里的接待员，一个守时又称职的工作人员，基本上我的工作就是清点客人、签署表格，并确保没有人觉得自己像个被排斥的文盲。总有可能某个工作狂设计师或某位保守的男爵迟到，但我不敢让日程恶化。

今年冠军的对手是一位来自遥远联邦的一流无产阶级棋手，一个顽强而宽宏大量的人。他小时候得过支气管炎，摔断过大腿骨，一度看似遭受种种不幸的牺牲品。人们曾怀疑他会神经崩溃，状态糟糕，信心无法挽回。然而他拒绝逃避这场较量；在球迷们默默的团结一致之下，他把每一场比赛都变成了奇迹。

决赛的风暴来临之前，大厅气氛紧张。我毫不夸张地说，空气既令人昏昏欲睡又仿佛带电，好像有某种预兆悬在台上。一连串官员，既讲究外交手腕又需要秩序，努力厘清规则、调节渐起的喧嚣。一位心不在焉的伯爵不停地摆弄一颗核桃，而冠军的一位姨母剥开一块蝴蝶形的点心分给众人。运送那座刻字奖杯的货运付了额外费用，任何迟到报名一律拒绝，绝无更改；委员会不会搁置任何申诉。

比赛本身看起来是无穷的乐趣。每位棋手都会拉伸思维，紧绷得像一根弹簧，时钟的刺激让每一步都因风险而显得粗糙。蛋白棒和奢侈的巧克力无人去碰。当挑战者迫使他的车合并、冠军防线上突然出现一丝破绽时，一阵荒谬而欢乐的喧嚣爆发了。那是纯粹的乐趣，连一个从隔壁动物园之行溜进来的小孩都看得懂。

结果的针对性人人明了。回家的航班将载着一位新英雄。当棋手们在记分表上签名时，我感受到平凡一天变得非凡的乐趣。老冠军的一位侍从在座位上昏昏欲睡，醒来鼓掌。有人说一个王朝结束了；也有人坚持那只是经历了一次巩固。这里没有阴谋，没有亵渎的把戏，没有对体育的拙劣模仿，只有一场干净的胜利。外面的快车路上车流满满，当当天报纸的纸浆带着角落里我的照片从印刷机上飞旋而出时，我让自己破例地，单纯地惊叹一回，把疑虑隔离在一旁。 因为现有的引擎几乎无法启动，我们便把它盖上毯子留在谷仓里。我会剥一个橙子，用平静的声音写一封信，小心不去打扰任何人。一种科学的管理精神应当渗透每一页，而不是让它空白，然而一件孩子的玩具被遗忘在寒气渗入木板的角落。
